content.text: What it is
The observability platform team ownership model

is an organisational pattern in which a dedicated, centralised team owns and operates the shared observability infrastructure — collectors, storage backends, dashboards, alerting infrastructure, and instrumentation libraries — while product engineering teams consume this infrastructure as tenants. The platform team sets standards (semantic conventions, span schemas, alert templates) and enforces them through SDK defaults and code review; product teams own the business logic of what to instrument, what SLOs to set, and what alerts to configure within the platform's boundaries. This division of responsibility prevents each engineering team from solving the same infrastructure problems independently, while still giving teams autonomy over their observability signals.
Why it matters

Agentic AI systems present unique observability challenges: spans span multiple services (orchestrator, LLM gateway, tool servers, memory stores), traces can run for hours, and AI-specific signals like token cost and model latency require custom instrumentation that standard APM tools do not provide out of the box. Without a platform team, each engineering team independently builds and maintains its own collector pipelines, dashboards, and retention policies — creating inconsistent signal quality, duplicated costs, and incompatible data models that make cross-service debugging impossible. A platform team establishes a common substrate that makes every agent service observable from day one with minimal per-team effort.
Benefits
Consistent span schema across all agent services enables cross-service trace correlation and organisation-wide dashboards.
Economies of scale: a single collector cluster, storage backend, and Grafana instance serves dozens of teams at a fraction of the per-team cost.
Compliance controls (retention, redaction, access) are enforced once in the platform rather than duplicated per team.
New agent services inherit observability baseline (traces, metrics, alerts) by adopting the platform SDK — no from-scratch setup.
Platform team can apply cross-cutting improvements (tail sampling, cost reduction, cardinality management) without requiring every product team to act.
Trade-offs and limitations
Platform becomes a bottleneck: teams must request features or schema changes through the platform team's roadmap and backlog.
Shared infrastructure failures (collector outage, storage backend degradation) affect all agent services simultaneously.
Schema standardisation requires upfront negotiation across teams, which is slow and political at large organisations.
Platform team needs deep expertise in both observability tools and AI-specific signals — a rare skill combination.
Multi-tenancy adds complexity to cost attribution, access control, and cardinality management.
How it works
The platform team operates the shared OTel Collector fleet, configures the storage backends (Tempo, Prometheus, Loki), and maintains Grafana. It publishes an
instrumentation SDK

— a thin wrapper around the official OTel SDK — that pre-configures exporters, sets required span attributes (service name, environment, team), and enforces semantic convention compliance at initialisation time. Product teams import this SDK rather than the bare OTel SDK.
The platform team defines
tenant namespaces

in Grafana and Prometheus to isolate each team's data within the shared backend. Teams self-serve their dashboards and alert rules within their namespace using GitOps-managed Grafana provisioning files. The platform team reviews namespace changes via pull request to enforce schema compliance without becoming a manual approval gate.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Observability platform ownership model: central platform team provides shared infrastructure, product teams and ML team consume as tenants, security team has read-only access

content.text: The platform team owns and operates all shared infrastructure components. Product teams are multi-tenants who embed the platform SDK, define their own SLOs and alert rules, and consume the shared dashboards and storage. Security and compliance teams have read-only access to audit traces and configure retention policies through the platform team's change process.
When to use it
Organisations with 5 or more engineering teams each deploying agent services — coordination costs exceed platform team overhead at this scale.
When cross-service trace correlation is required — debugging multi-agent workflows spanning several teams.
When compliance or security teams need centralised access control and audit trail management across all agent services.
When observability cost is significant enough to justify a dedicated team to manage it (typically at tens of millions of spans per minute).
How to implement
Define the team charter: what infrastructure the platform team owns, what product teams own, and what is shared (semantic conventions, alert templates).
Build and publish an internal instrumentation SDK that wraps OTel SDK with platform defaults, semantic convention enforcement, and the OTLP endpoint for the shared collector.
Deploy the shared OTel Collector fleet with high availability (minimum 3 replicas), and configure per-team routing using the
routing
connector keyed on the
service.team
span attribute.
Provision Grafana tenants (organisations or folders) for each product team with RBAC scoped to their namespace.
Publish an onboarding runbook and self-service portal where new teams register their service names, declare their SLO targets, and receive a pre-configured dashboard template.
Define and publish an SLA for the platform itself: ingestion lag, query latency, uptime. Put the platform on call for its own SLA breaches.
Establish a quarterly platform roadmap review with stakeholder input from product teams — prevents the backlog from becoming dominated by one team's requests.
Examples

# Internal platform SDK wrapper — published as internal package
# pip install internal-observability-sdk
from internal_obs_sdk import configure_observability

# All teams call this at startup; platform defaults are pre-wired
configure_observability(
    service_name="customer-support-agent",
    service_version="2.4.1",
    environment="production",
    team="customer-experience",
    # Optional: override collector endpoint for regional deployments
    # collector_endpoint="https://otel.us-east-1.internal:4317"
)

# configure_observability internally does:
# 1. Detects OTLP endpoint from environment or default
# 2. Sets required resource attributes (service.team, deployment.environment)
# 3. Enforces semantic convention attribute names
# 4. Registers BatchSpanProcessor with retry
# 5. Installs OTel instrumentation for common libraries (httpx, sqlalchemy)

# GitOps: product team self-serves alert rules in their namespace
# File: grafana/alerts/customer-experience/agent-error-rate.yaml
apiVersion: 1
groups:
  - orgId: 1
    name: customer-experience-agent-alerts
    folder: customer-experience  # scoped to team namespace
    interval: 1m
    rules:
      - title: Agent error rate high
        condition: C
        data:
          - refId: A
            datasourceUid: prometheus
            model:
              expr: |
                rate(agent_errors_total{service_team="customer-experience"}[5m])
                /
                rate(agent_requests_total{service_team="customer-experience"}[5m])
        noDataState: NoData
        annotations:
          summary: "Agent error rate above 1%"
          runbook: "https://runbooks.internal/agent-error-rate"
Real-world example

A large financial services firm builds ten distinct agent products across four engineering divisions. Prior to forming a platform team, each division operated its own Prometheus, Jaeger, and Grafana stacks — resulting in 00k/year of duplicated tooling spend, incompatible trace formats that prevented cross-division debugging, and inconsistent retention policies that created compliance audit findings.

After staffing a four-person observability platform team, the firm consolidates onto a shared OTel Collector fleet, a single Tempo cluster, and a multi-tenant Grafana. The platform team publishes a Python and TypeScript SDK wrapper that all ten agent products adopt over six months. Tooling spend falls to 20k/year. Cross-division trace correlation is available immediately for any trace that carries the standard span attributes. Compliance retention is enforced uniformly by a single pipeline, eliminating the audit finding.
Common mistakes
Platform team becomes an approval bottleneck for every dashboard and alert change, defeating the self-service goal. Use GitOps with PR review instead of manual approval.
Under-staffing the platform team: one person cannot reliably maintain shared infrastructure for 50+ agent services with on-call rotation.
Allowing product teams to bypass the shared collector with direct-to-storage instrumentation, creating schema drift.
Publishing a mandatory SDK update without adequate deprecation period, breaking production instrumentation for downstream teams.
No SLA for the platform itself: if the platform team does not commit to uptime and latency, product teams lose trust and build workarounds.
Best practices
Define and publish the platform SLA before onboarding the first tenant — SLAs set expectations and give the platform team clear success criteria.
Use GitOps for all tenant configurations (dashboard provisioning, alert rules, retention policies) — changes are reviewable, auditable, and rollbackable.
Ship a zero-configuration SDK: teams should be observable with one function call, not ten pages of documentation.
Run the platform team's own services through the shared platform — eat your own dog food to catch friction before tenants hit it.
Hold a quarterly platform roadmap review with representatives from each major tenant team; prevent the backlog from being captured by one team's needs.
Publish per-team cost attribution dashboards so product teams can see their share of observability spend and self-govern cardinality usage.
Performance and scalability

The shared collector fleet must scale to handle the aggregate span throughput of all tenant teams with appropriate head room. Deploy the OTel Collector in a Kubernetes deployment with horizontal pod autoscaling keyed on CPU and
otelcol_receiver_accepted_spans

. The collector is stateless (in head-sampling mode) so scaling is straightforward; tail-sampling deployments require sticky routing and scale more carefully. Provision storage backends with multi-AZ replication and test failover quarterly. The platform SLA should include a documented RTO and RPO for each storage backend tier.
Tooling and ecosystem
Layer
Tool

Collection
OTel Collector
Operate fleet, configure pipelines
Trace storage
Grafana Tempo
Operate, manage retention
Metrics storage
Prometheus / Thanos
Operate, set cardinality limits
Dashboards / alerting
Grafana
Provision tenants, enforce RBAC
SDK distribution
Internal package registry
Publish versioned SDK packages
Comparison with alternatives
Model
Consistency
Autonomy
Cost
Shared platform team
High — enforced by SDK
Medium — within standards
Low — shared infra
Per-team ownership
Low — schema drift
High
High — duplicated stacks
Managed SaaS (Datadog, Honeycomb)
Medium — product-enforced
High — self-service UI
Very high at volume
Security and reliability

The shared platform's RBAC model must prevent tenant teams from reading each other's traces, metrics, or logs. In Grafana, use folder-level permissions so that team A cannot see team B's dashboards. In Tempo, use namespace-based label routing and query-time label enforcement. Audit cross-tenant data access attempts in the platform's own access logs.

The platform's collector fleet is a critical dependency — if it fails, all agent services lose observability simultaneously. Operate the fleet with N+2 redundancy, multi-AZ placement, and a circuit breaker that drops spans rather than blocking agent processes when the collector is overloaded. The application should never wait on the observability pipeline.
Platform team staffing and skill model

At early scale (fewer than 20 agent services), a single embedded platform engineer within an SRE or infrastructure team is sufficient. As the number of services grows past 50, a dedicated team of three to six engineers becomes necessary to maintain the platform SLA, develop the SDK, onboard new tenants, and handle on-call incidents. The team needs expertise in:
OTel Collector configuration, pipeline design, and performance tuning.
Prometheus and Thanos for metrics at scale, including cardinality management and recording rules.
AI-specific observability signals (token cost, model latency, embedding distance) — general-purpose SREs rarely have this background.
Kubernetes operations for deploying and scaling the collector fleet and storage backends.
Developer experience: building SDKs, writing documentation, and supporting tenants during onboarding.
Managing cardinality and cost in a multi-tenant platform

High-cardinality span attributes are the primary cause of Prometheus out-of-memory incidents in shared platforms. A single team that adds a high-cardinality label (like a full URL with query parameters) to all their spans can degrade the shared Prometheus for every other tenant. Enforce cardinality limits per tenant using Prometheus
label_limit
and
sample_limit
per scrape job, or use VictoriaMetrics' per-tenant cardinality limits. Publish a cardinality usage dashboard so teams can see their own contribution and self-correct before hitting limits.

# Prometheus scrape config: cardinality limit per tenant job
scrape_configs:
  - job_name: customer-experience-agent
    sample_limit: 50000       # max active series from this job
    label_limit: 30           # max labels per metric
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['customer-agent-metrics:9090']
    relabel_configs:
      # Enforce service.team label is set and correct
      - source_labels: [__meta_kubernetes_pod_label_team]
        target_label: service_team
        action: replace
Key takeaways
A centralised platform team that owns shared observability infrastructure is justified when 5 or more engineering teams each deploy agent services.
The platform team owns infrastructure and standards; product teams own SLOs, alerts, and dashboards within those standards.
Publish a zero-configuration internal SDK so that adopting the platform requires one function call, not a week of plumbing.
Use GitOps for tenant configurations — self-service with PR review, not manual approval queues.
The platform must have its own SLA and on-call rotation — it is production infrastructure, not a best-effort internal tool.
Multi-tenancy requires RBAC isolation between teams, cost attribution dashboards, and cardinality limits per tenant to prevent one team from degrading the shared platform.
